chunks.text: Not Just Pedigree Part Two Twelve Lessons From Good Dogs About Family Pressure and Freedom By Kyung Suk Yi and Jin Sung Kim © Kyung Suk Yi and Jin Sung Kim What makes you valuable? Is it your last name? Your parents’ jobs? The awards on your family’s wall? Or is it something deeper, something that has nothing to do with where you come from? Meet twelve dogs who know that pedigree doesn’t determine their worth. A shelter mutt can love just as fiercely as a champion show dog. A Lab doesn’t need the right friends to be happy. A German Shepherd can serve without following his family’s footsteps.

Through their stories, you’ll meet young (and not- so-young) people like you who are learning to navigate their family expectations, cultural traditions, and the weight of being “special.” You’ll discover that the pressure to be perfect, to carry on traditions, or to live up to your family name doesn’t have to define you. Because here’s the truth: God doesn’t check your pedigree before He loves you. 2 The proceeds from this book goes to: The Never Alone Mission ℅ A&K 440 N. Wolfe Rd. Sunnyvale, CA 94085

Front Cover Photo: Robot Photo unsplash.com/@robotphoto 3 For all the young (and not-so-young) people carrying the weight of heritage, may you find freedom in love. For all the families learning to hold tradition with grace, may you find peace. And for every reader who needed to hear that their worth isn’t tied to meeting every expectation, this book is for you. 4 Introduction 6 Lesson 7: The Invisible Child 9 Lesson 8: The Burden Bearer 23 Lesson 9: The Guard Who Wandered 34

Welcome back. Or, if this is your first time joining us, welcome. This book is the second part of a longer story. In Part One, we met six dogs and their families navigating the pressures that come with strong family identities. We watched a Shiba Inu learn to perform whenever guests arrived. We sat with a German Shepherd whose gentle nature didn’t match his rescue dog lineage. We walked alongside a missionary family whose Australian Shepherd reflected their own hidden stress. We saw a young baseball player whose worth had become tangled up with his statistics.

Those stories explored how family pressure shows up. We saw what happens to a young person who is always being watched and measured. We watched families try to live up to standards that no one could actually meet. And we saw the exhaustion that comes from always having to perform. If you read Part One, you know what we mean. If you didn’t, you can still find your footing here. 6 The struggles in these pages will feel familiar to anyone who knows what it’s like to carry other people’s expectations.

Part Two continues with six more dogs and their families. The lessons ahead look at what happens inside us when we’ve been carrying that weight for a long time. These stories explore the quieter wounds that we don’t always talk about. We’ll meet a young woman who feels invisible even though her parents are famous for helping others. We’ll sit with a twelve-year-old boy exhausted from being the “good child” while his sister gets all the attention for her rebellion. We’ll watch a young woman in Egypt wrestle with guilt for simply being human, for needing rest, for having moments when she couldn’t keep up.

And we’ll find our way toward freedom. One of us, the deacon, has been living with dogs almost all his life. The other, the layperson, has walked through the unique pressures of family heritage. Together, we’ve learned that dogs don’t measure each other’s worth by bloodlines or breeding certificates. They just love. And they’ve taught us that God does the same. 7 These stories won’t fix everything. Family dynamics are complicated, and there are no simple answers. But we hope they offer something valuable. We hope you’ll read these stories and feel like someone understands what you’ve been going through. And maybe, by the end, you’ll find a little more room to breathe.

So whether you’re the child of immigrants carrying the weight of their sacrifice, the heir to a family business who’d rather be doing something else, the pastor’s kid who’s tired of performing, or simply someone who has wondered whether you’re loved for who you are or for what you represent, these pages are for you. You’ve already taken the first step by picking up this book. Let’s keep walking together. 8

The daughter sat in the back row of the youth group meeting, watching her parents deliver their testimony to a room full of eager faces. The projector displayed photos of coding bootcamp graduates, young people from underserved communities whose lives had been transformed through her parents’ nonprofit. “God called us from success to significance,” the father explained, his Pakistani accent barely noticeable after twenty years in Silicon Valley. “We could have stayed at our tech companies, but God had bigger plans.”

The mother continued seamlessly. “Now we teach underprivileged youth to code with purpose. To use technology for God’s kingdom.” The audience erupted in applause. The daughter had heard this presentation dozens of times. She could recite it word for word, including the pause points for effect and the moment when the mother 9 would tear up describing their first graduate who now worked at a major tech company. Their Great Pyrenees, Ghost, lay beside the daughter’s chair. With his massive frame and thick white coat, he should have been impossible to miss. Yet people regularly tripped over him. His white fur blended with the cream-colored walls. The daughter understood the feeling.

After the presentation, the familiar routine began. Youth group members and their parents swarmed the couple with questions about internships, college recommendations, and spiritual advice. A few adults noticed the daughter and approached with the same predictable comments. “You must be so proud of your parents!” “Following in their footsteps, I’m sure?” “What a blessing to have such godly examples!” An older mother from church cornered the daughter near the refreshment table. “Your parents are incredible. That article in the Christian magazine about them was so inspiring. You’re a senior, right? You must tell me which top schools

10 you’re considering. I’m sure with your parents’ connections…” “I’m actually a junior,” the daughter said quietly. “And I haven’t really thought…” “Oh, but you must be thinking about it! With parents like yours, I’m sure you’ve been coding since elementary school. My son could really use some guidance. Perhaps your parents could mentor him?” Before the daughter could respond, the woman had already moved on to intercept the mother, leaving the daughter standing alone by the hummus and pita chips. Ghost pressed against her leg, his presence solid but unnoticed by everyone else in the room.

At home that evening, the family gathered for dinner. Photos from nonprofit events hung on the dining room walls, alongside awards from various Christian organizations and a signed letter from a government official commending their work. “A family from church wants to fund a new cohort in a neighboring city,” the father said, serving the biryani the mother had somehow found time to 11 make. “They were impressed by our graduation statistics.” “That’s wonderful,” the mother responded. “God continues to open doors. Oh, the neighbor asked if you could tutor her son in Python. I said you’d email her.”

The daughter pushed her rice around her plate. “I don’t really know Python that well.” Her parents exchanged glances. “You’ve been around code your whole life,” the father said gently. “Don’t sell yourself short.” “Being around something doesn’t mean I absorbed it by osmosis,” the daughter replied. “I’m actually more interested in—“ Her phone buzzed with a text from the youth pastor: “Your parents’ testimony was POWERFUL tonight! You’re so lucky to have such amazing role models!” The daughter excused herself and went to her room. Ghost padded silently behind her. She sat at her desk, surrounded by her actual interests: sketches of graphic novels she’d been working on,

12 creative writing notebooks, and a collection of contemporary poetry. None of which anyone ever asked about. She opened her laptop to find seventeen professional networking notifications. Ever since her parents had created a profile for her “to build her professional network early,” she received constant connection requests from people who wanted access to her parents. The messages were always the same: “I’d love to connect and learn more about your parents’ nonprofit!” Ghost settled on her bedroom floor, taking up most of the available space. Even in the confined room, his white fur seemed to fade into the cream carpet.

The next day at school, the daughter sat in her AP Literature class, the one place where she felt most like herself. The teacher was discussing the woman at the well from John 4, part of their “Biblical Literature as Literature” unit. “What’s fascinating about this encounter,” the teacher said, “is that Jesus chose to have his longest recorded theological discussion with someone who was intentionally invisible. She came to the well at 13 noon, the hottest part of the day, specifically to avoid other people.”

The daughter looked up from her notes. The teacher continued, “The other villagers saw her only through the lens of her relationships, her failures, her status as a Samaritan. But Jesus saw her as a complete person worthy of the deepest spiritual truths. He revealed His identity as the Messiah to her before anyone else. To religious leaders? No. To crowds? No. To one invisible woman at a well.” The daughter’s pen stopped. She gripped it tighter as tears blurred her vision.

“The most profound part,” the teacher added, “is when Jesus tells her, ‘If you knew the gift of God and who it is that asks you for a drink, you would have asked him and he would have given you living water.’ He’s saying He knows her completely. He created her. He sees her essence, her real thirst, her actual needs.” After class, the daughter lingered. The teacher looked up from her desk. “Did you have a question?” 14 “I was thinking about the woman at the well,” the daughter said carefully. “Everyone knew about her, but nobody really knew her. Until Jesus.”

The teacher studied her for a moment. “That’s very insightful. You know, in all my years teaching, I’ve noticed you have a real gift for literary analysis. Your creative writing pieces are exceptional too. Have you thought about what you want to study in college?” The daughter blinked. It was the first time an adult had asked about her future without assuming the answer. “I actually love writing. Graphic novels, specifically. I’ve been working on one about identity and belonging.”

The teacher’s eyes lit up. “That’s wonderful! There are some excellent programs for that. Many universities have incredible creative writing programs with visual narrative tracks.” “My parents don’t really know,” the daughter admitted. “Everyone assumes I’ll do computer science.” 15 The teacher leaned back in her chair. “Can I share something? I’ve taught hundreds of students over the years. Many of them were children of successful people: tech executives, venture capitalists, university professors. The ones who thrived weren’t necessarily the ones who followed their parents’ paths. They were the ones who figured out their own calling.”

She paused, then added, “The woman at the well… after Jesus saw her, really saw her, she ran back to town and told everyone about Him. She said, ‘Come see a man who told me everything I ever did.’ Her story. Her truth.” That evening, the daughter found herself at another nonprofit fundraising event at their church. She stood near the silent auction table while her parents worked the room. Ghost, wearing his therapy dog vest, stood beside her like a white shadow. Her parents had gotten him certified for their nonprofit work.

A young woman approached to look at the auction items. She glanced down at Ghost, then did a double-take. 16 “Oh my goodness, is this a Great Pyrenees? He’s magnificent!” The woman knelt down. “What’s his name?” “Ghost,” the daughter replied, surprised. The woman laughed. “That’s ironic for such a huge dog!” She looked up. “Is he yours?” “Yes, well, my family’s.” “He’s beautiful. I had Pyrenees growing up. They’re such gentle souls. They have this way of just being there with you without demanding any attention.” The woman stood up. “I’m new to the church. Just moved here from another state.”

The daughter introduced herself. The woman’s eyes widened slightly. “Oh, are you related to the couple who run this program?” The daughter’s heart sank. “They’re my parents.” But the woman surprised her. “That must be interesting. Everyone probably assumes they know everything about you based on what your parents do.” 17 The daughter stared at her. “Yes. Exactly.” The woman smiled knowingly. “My mom was a federal judge. Everyone assumed I’d go to law school. I’m actually a graphic designer. Took years for people to stop asking when I was going to ‘get serious’ about my career.” She paused. “What do you enjoy doing?”

For the next twenty minutes, the daughter found herself talking about her graphic novel, her love of visual storytelling, her poetry collection. The woman listened, asking questions about the creative process, influences, dreams. “You know,” the woman said finally, “I have a friend who works for a publishing house that specializes in graphic novels. If you ever want someone to look at your work, not because of who your parents are, but because you’re a young artist, I could connect you.”

A smile spread across the daughter’s face. “That would be amazing.” The woman handed her a business card. “The thing about being invisible,” she said gently, “is that it sometimes takes one person really seeing 18 you to remember that you were always there. Valuable. Complete. Just like your Ghost here. He’s been magnificent this whole time. Someone just needed to notice.” That night, the daughter sat with her parents after the event. The fundraising had been successful, exceeding their goals.

“Mom, Dad,” she began. “I need to tell you something.” They looked up. “I don’t want to code. I want to be a graphic novelist. I’ve been working on a book for two years. My teacher thinks I have real talent. And tonight, I met someone who might help me get published.” Her parents were quiet. Then the mother asked, “Why didn’t you tell us?” “Because everyone, including you, sees me as an extension of the nonprofit. No one ever asks what I want. They assume I’ll follow your path.”

The father set down his chai. “We never meant…” 19 “I know,” the daughter interrupted. “I know you love me. But sometimes I feel like Ghost. I’m here, I’m obvious, but somehow invisible. People see ‘the daughter’ but they don’t see me.” The mother moved to sit beside her. “We’ve been so focused on teaching other families to really listen to their kids. To ask what they want. To help kids who feel invisible. And right here, in our own home, we never asked you about your own dreams.”

The father added quietly, “In the Bible, God says He knows us by name. Not by who our parents are or what they do. By our own names, each person individually. He doesn’t see you as ‘our daughter who should follow our path.’ He sees you. Your own person. Your own calling. We forgot to see you that way.” “There’s this story,” the daughter said, “about Jesus and a woman at a well. Everyone knew about her, but Jesus was the first person to really see her. To talk to her about real things. To treat her as a whole person worthy of truth.” She looked at her parents. “I need you to see me that way. As a whole person.”

20 The mother pulled her into a hug, and the daughter felt her tears. “We see you, sweetheart. We see you.” Over the following weeks, her parents started asking about her graphic novel. The father even offered to help with the technical aspects of digital illustration, supporting her actual interests. The mother began introducing her differently at events: “This is our daughter. She’s a talented writer and artist.” Ghost still lay quietly in corners, his white fur blending with the walls. The daughter noticed him now in a different way. His steady presence. His quiet companionship. He had always been there.

One evening, as the daughter worked on her graphic novel with Ghost sprawled across her entire bedroom floor, she thought about the woman at the well. After Jesus saw her, really saw her, He didn’t condemn her. He knew about the five husbands, the searching for love in all the wrong places. And He offered her living water, the fulfillment she’d been seeking all along. The woman was fully known, but she didn’t have to hide anymore. She was known. She was seen. And she was loved.

21 The daughter thought about this as she worked. We can bring everything to Jesus. What we really want to do with our lives. Our disappointments in the people who love us but don’t seem to know the real us. The pressure from parents or from others to go one way, when we desperately want to go a different way. Our way. He sees it all. He knows it all. And He is always with us. He has been. He is now. And He always will be.

The same Jesus who saw that woman saw the daughter too. The writer. The artist. The complete person He had created her to be. She looked at Ghost, and he looked at her. “We were always here,” she told him. “Jesus always saw us. Now others are beginning to see us too.” And for the first time in years, that felt like enough. 22

Atlas, a Bernese Mountain Dog, lived with a Filipino family in San Antonio. The family also had Buddy, a Beagle. The two dogs were as different as dogs could be. Every morning, Atlas waited patiently by the door for his breakfast. He sat perfectly still until the father set down his bowl. Then Atlas ate slowly and carefully, never spilling a single piece of kibble. Buddy, on the other hand, bounced around the kitchen the moment he smelled food. He whined and spun in circles. When the mother finally put down his bowl, Buddy dove into it so fast that kibble scattered across the floor. He’d gobble up every piece, including the ones that rolled under the refrigerator.

“Good boy, Atlas,” the father would say, scratching behind the big dog’s ears. “You’re such a good boy.” 23 To Buddy, the mother would sigh, “Why can’t you be more like Atlas?” This pattern repeated throughout the day. When visitors came to the door, Atlas stood calmly beside the father, his massive body still and dignified. Buddy barked and jumped, his tail wagging so hard his whole back end moved with it. “Atlas, you’re perfect,” visitors would say, petting his broad head.

Buddy would get shuffled into the backyard. “Sorry,” the mother would apologize. “He’s still learning.” The family had a twelve-year-old son and a seventeen-year-old daughter. The son watched the dogs carefully. He noticed how Atlas always got praised. How the father’s hand always found Atlas’s head for scratching. How people at church would comment on what a “well-trained, dignified dog” Atlas was. He also noticed how Buddy always seemed to be in trouble. How the mother’s voice would get tired when she said his name. How Buddy spent more time in the backyard than Atlas did.

24 One afternoon, the son found Buddy in the backyard, digging a hole under the fence. His little beagle body was covered in dirt, his tail wagging furiously. “Buddy, no!” the son called. Buddy looked up, tongue hanging out, completely happy. He trotted over to the son, leaving dirt prints across the patio. The son sighed and got the hose to clean him off. Later that evening, the daughter came home late. Very late. The son lay in bed and heard the argument start downstairs. The daughter had been out with friends. She smelled like alcohol. The parents were worried and angry.

Atlas, who had been sleeping in the hallway, stood up. His big paws padded down the stairs. Buddy, who slept in the son’s room, lifted his head and whined softly. The son couldn’t sleep through the shouting. He crept to his door and looked down the stairs. He could see into the living room where his parents and sister were talking. His sister was crying. His 25 mother looked exhausted. His father sat with his head in his hands. Atlas walked into the room. The big dog moved to the father, who sat on the couch with his head in his hands. Atlas pressed his body against the father’s legs. The father’s hand went to Atlas’s head.

The son could see his father looked exhausted. The respected church elder, worn out and defeated. His sister sat across the room, her face in her hands, crying. The dog didn’t move. He just stood there, close against the father. Buddy appeared at the top of the stairs next to the son. The little beagle whined again, looking down at the family. His tail wasn’t wagging. He seemed confused by all the sadness. The son picked up Buddy and carried him back to bed. The beagle curled up against the son’s chest, warm and solid.

“You’re always getting in trouble,” the son whispered to Buddy. “But I still love you.” Buddy licked the son’s chin. 26 The son thought about that. Buddy got scolded multiple times a day. Buddy knocked over trash cans and dug holes and barked too much and ate things he wasn’t supposed to eat. But the son loved him anyway. When Buddy came bounding up to him after school, tail wagging like crazy, the son always smiled. He loved Buddy not because Buddy was good, but because Buddy was his.

The next morning, the family sat at the kitchen table. The daughter’s eyes were red and puffy. The son’s parents looked tired. Atlas lay under the table, his bulk touching everyone’s feet. Buddy sat by his food bowl, looking hopeful. The mother fed both dogs. “Good boy, Atlas,” she said automatically. Then she looked at Buddy, who was already bouncing with excitement. She paused. “Good boy, Buddy,” she said softly. Buddy’s whole body wiggled with joy at the praise. The father cleared his throat. “We need to talk as a family,” he said. He looked at the daughter. “About last night. About a lot of things.”

The daughter stared at her plate. 27 “When I was twelve,” the father said, looking at the son, “I tried so hard to be perfect. To make my parents proud. My younger brother was always in trouble, and I thought if I was good enough, it would make up for his mistakes. I got sick from the stress. Actual stomach ulcers.” The son stared at his father. The perfect church elder had been stressed and sick? “Your uncle was always in trouble,” the father continued, now looking at the daughter. “He failed almost all of his classes. School nights, weekends. Didn’t matter. He was out every night. Until really late. Sometimes coming back home in the morning. He got into all sorts of trouble. Got into a lot of fights. But my parents never stopped loving him. They never gave up. And eventually, he found his way.”

Under the table, Atlas rested his head on the father’s foot. “This morning, I watched how we treat our dogs,” the father said. “Atlas gets praised for everything. Buddy gets scolded for everything. But watch this.” 28 He stood up and walked to the door. Both dogs immediately followed. Atlas walked calmly. Buddy bounced and wiggled. The father opened the door to let them into the backyard. “Atlas is obedient,” the father said. “That’s his nature. It’s easier to live with. But Buddy…” He watched the beagle immediately start sniffing around the yard, his nose to the ground, following some fascinating scent. “Buddy is joyful. He loves life. He gets into trouble because he’s curious and excited about everything.”

The father turned back to his family. “I love them both the same. When Atlas is old and can’t walk well anymore, I’ll love him. When Buddy digs up the garden again tomorrow, I’ll still love him. They’re both my dogs.” He sat back down. “You’re both my children. I don’t love you more or less based on your choices. You’re mine.” He paused. “But even more than that, you’re His. The Lord gave both of you to us to care for. For a short while. We love you because you’re ours. But God loves you because you’re His. And His love is bigger than ours will ever be.”

29 The daughter started crying again. “I don’t even believe in God anymore.” The mother reached across the table. “Then we’ll believe for you until you find your way. You’re still our daughter. And you’re still God’s child.” The father looked at the son. “And you don’t have to be perfect. You can be yourself. Make mistakes. Learn who you are. We’ll love you the same.” That Sunday, the daughter stayed home from church. The parents didn’t make excuses. They simply said, “Pray for our family.”

At church, the pastor talked to the son after service. “You’re such a good example,” the pastor said. “Your parents must be so grateful to have at least one child who honors them.” The son looked at his parents. His father’s hand was on Atlas’s head. The big dog stood calmly beside them. The son thought about Buddy, who was home in the backyard, probably digging another hole or barking at squirrels. 30 “My sister honors them too,” the son said. “She’s going through some things right now. But mom and dad love us both.”

The pastor looked surprised. The son’s father smiled slightly and squeezed his shoulder. That evening, the family sat down for dinner together. Even the daughter came to the table, though she’d spent most of the day in her room. The father read from the Bible about a father who had two sons. One son left home and wasted everything. The other son stayed and worked hard. But the father loved them both. “The son who left got a party when he came home,” the father read. “The son who stayed got angry about that. But the father told him, ‘Everything I have is yours. You’re always with me. Your brother was lost, and now he’s found.’”

Under the table, both dogs lay near each other. Atlas’s massive form took up most of the space. Buddy was curled up against him, snoring softly. “The father didn’t love one son more than the other,” the father explained. “Both sons were lost in different ways. One was lost out in the world. 31 The other was lost right there at home, thinking he had to earn love by being good.” The son looked at his sister. “I’m sorry,” he said suddenly. “For being the good one all the time. For making it harder for you.”

His sister reached over and gave him a noogie on the head. She smiled a little through her tears. “You didn’t mean to.” “I’m going to try to just be myself,” the son said. “I’m tired of trying to be perfect.” “And I’m going to try to stop causing so many problems,” the sister said. The father reached down and petted both dogs. “Just be yourselves. That’s all we’ve ever wanted.” Later that night, the son found both dogs on the back porch. Atlas lay in his usual spot, alert and calm. Buddy was sniffing around the edges of the porch, his nose working overtime.

The son sat down on the porch. Atlas rested close to him. Buddy stopped sniffing and bounded over, trying to climb into the son’s lap. 32 Two completely different dogs. One obedient and praised. One mischievous and corrected. But both loved. Both part of the family. Both valued for who they were, not what they did. The son thought about God. How God must look at all His children the way the family looked at their two dogs. Some people were like Atlas, steady and obedient. Others were like Buddy, messy and impulsive. But God loved them all. He didn’t love the “good” ones more. He didn’t love the “troublemakers” less.

God’s love wasn’t something you earned by being perfect. It was something you received by being His. Atlas huffed softly. Buddy yawned and stretched in his lap. Both dogs were content. Both dogs were home. Both dogs were loved.ß 33

Kalbawi, the Armant sheepdog, lifted his shaggy head from where he’d been dozing by the pond’s edge. The afternoon heat had lulled him into sleep when he should have been watching for herons and monitor lizards. A car approached the fish farm, kicking up dust on the access road, and he scrambled to his feet. His tail went rigid and his ears perked forward. The daughter recognized the familiar sight: Kalbawi caught sleeping on the job, now standing at attention as if nothing had happened.

The daughter stood in the shade of the palm trees with her father, checking the aerators. Her stomach tightened as she recognized her mother’s white sedan. These surprise visits during work hours never meant anything good. “Kalbawi was sleeping again,” the daughter said quietly. 34 Her father glanced at the dog, who stood stiffly at attention despite having just scrambled up from sleep. “He works hard at night when the real predators come. Let him rest in the heat.” His voice carried the patience of someone who understood that not everything could be controlled.

The mother stepped out of the car, her mission organization’s lanyard still around her neck, her face set in the expression the daughter had come to dread. “We need to talk,” she announced, not bothering with greetings. “A woman from church called me at work. At work. Her daughter saw you at the cinema with that boy. The Muslim boy.” “His name is…” the daughter started, then caught herself. “And he’s not really Muslim. His family doesn’t practice.” “That makes it worse!” The mother’s voice rose. “At least a devout Muslim would understand faith. This boy believes in nothing.”

As the mother’s voice rose, Kalbawi’s ears flattened. He began to pace along the pond’s perimeter, the way he did when storms approached or strangers came too close. His 35 whines grew louder. The father watched the dog circle anxiously, searching for whatever threat had disturbed his family but finding nothing to chase away. The father set down his net-mending tools slowly. “Go feed the fish in the far pond. Your mother and I need to discuss this.” “But I…”

“Go.” The daughter walked toward the smaller pond, Kalbawi trailing behind her. She could still hear her parents’ voices carrying across the water. “She’s destroying our reputation,” the mother said. “I work for a missions organization. How can I talk about bringing people to Christ when my own daughter is running around with a non- believer? What will people think?” “Perhaps,” the father said carefully, “they will think we are human.” “This isn’t a joke!” 36 “I’m not joking. Remember when we were young? Your parents forbade you from seeing me because I was just a fisherman’s son.”

“That was different. We were both Christians.” “Were we? Or was I just someone who went to church because that’s what my family did?” The daughter sat by the far pond, absent-mindedly scattering feed pellets across the water. The tilapia rose in a frenzy of silver flashes. Kalbawi settled beside her, his warm bulk resting against her leg. “I don’t even know why I like him,” she whispered to the dog. “I know it’s wrong. The Bible says not to be unequally yoked. Mom quotes it every day now. But when I’m with him, I feel… seen. He asks me what I think, not what my church thinks. He wants to know my dreams, not my mother’s plans for me.”

Kalbawi’s ears perked up at a sound from the main pond. A heron had landed on the far bank, its long neck extended toward the water. The dog should have charged immediately, barking and chasing. It was his job. Instead, he looked at the bird, then at the daughter, then back at the bird. His muscles 37 tensed as if to spring forward, but he stayed frozen in place. He whined. “Go,” the daughter said. “It’s okay.” But Kalbawi leaned into her instead. The heron, emboldened, waded into the shallows.

That evening, the family gathered for dinner in tense silence. The mother had discovered Kalbawi’s negligence when she went to check the pond and found the heron casually fishing in their stock. “That dog is useless,” she said, spooning rice onto plates with sharp movements. “We feed him, shelter him, and he can’t do the one job he has. He should sleep outside tonight. Maybe hunger will teach him responsibility.” The father looked at Kalbawi, who was lying in his usual spot by the door, eyes moving between family members. “He was comforting her.”

“His job is to guard fish. We don’t keep him so he can abandon his post every time she gets upset. She is making bad choices. She doesn’t need comforting for that.” 38 “Perhaps,” the father said, reaching for the bread, “love is more important than duty sometimes.” The mother’s hand froze. “What are you saying?” “I’m saying the dog chose compassion over obligation. As Christ did when he stopped to heal on the Sabbath.” “Don’t you dare compare our daughter running around with a Muslim boy to Jesus healing the sick.”

“I’m not running around with him,” the daughter said. “We’re friends. We went to one movie.” “It starts with one movie,” the mother said. “Then it’s coffee. Then it’s meeting his family. Then you’re married to an unbeliever and your children grow up confused, not knowing what to believe.” “Maybe that’s better than growing up knowing exactly what to believe but not knowing why,” the daughter shot back. The silence that followed was deafening. Kalbawi went still, his panting stopped.

39 The mother stood abruptly. “I’m going to evening prayers. When I come back, I expect that dog outside and you to have deleted that boy’s number from your phone.” After she left, the father and daughter sat in the cooling evening air. The tension in the room eased. Kalbawi ventured closer, resting his head on the father’s knee. “Your mother loves you,” the father said finally. “She’s afraid.” “Of what? That I’ll embarrass her at work?” “That you’ll lose your faith. That you’ll suffer. That she’ll have failed as a mother.”

“I haven’t lost my faith. I just… I don’t understand it anymore. In the Bible, Paul talks about going through something similar. He kept struggling to do what he wanted to do and ended up doing the things he hated. That’s exactly how I feel. I know I shouldn’t have feelings for him. I try not to. But they’re there anyway. Does that make me bad?” The father’s weathered hand found Kalbawi’s head, stroking the coarse fur. “This morning, this

40 dog disobeyed me. Again. Every day I tell him to stay on shore. Every day he jumps in my boat. He knows it’s wrong. He does it anyway.” “But you don’t punish him.” “Because I understand him. He wasn’t made to watch from the shore. He was made to be with his master. His disobedience comes from love, not rebellion.” “Are you saying it’s okay for me to date him?” “I’m saying that feelings are not sins. Actions can be. But the feeling itself? That’s just being human. Like you said, even Paul struggled. The question isn’t whether you have these feelings. It’s what you choose to do with them.”

The next morning at the fish farm, Kalbawi suddenly stood alert. Another heron had landed by the pond. This time, he looked at the father and waited. “Go,” the father said gently. “Do your job.” The dog bounded off, barking as he charged toward the pond. The bird took flight with a 41 startled cry. Kalbawi chased it to the pond’s edge, his tail wagging in wide arcs. “Sometimes,” the father continued, “we need to feel understood before we can make the right choice. Your mother wants to force the right choice. But when you force someone to obey, that’s not faith. It’s just compliance.”

“So what do I do?” “You pray. You think. You be honest with yourself about why you’re drawn to this boy. Is it him, or is it escape from your mother’s expectations? And remember that even if you make the wrong choice, we have God’s mercy and His grace.” “Mom would never forgive me if I dated him.” “Your mother has forgiven worse in others. She counsels unwed mothers at the mission, former addicts, people who’ve made every mistake. She shows them grace daily. She just struggles to show it to you because she loves you too much to watch you struggle.”

That night, the daughter heard her parents talking in their room. Her mother had returned from 42 prayers calmer but still firm. Through the thin walls, she heard her father’s patient voice. “Remember when Kalbawi was a puppy? He chewed your favorite shoes, the ones from your sister in Cairo.” “Don’t remind me.” “You were so angry. You said we should give him away. But you didn’t. You bought bitter spray, moved your shoes higher, trained him patiently. Now he’s devoted to you.”

“A dog is not our daughter.” “No. But the principle is the same. When you punish harshly, all you get is fear. Or rebellion. But when you correct with patience and love, that’s when real wisdom grows.” “The Bible says…” “The Bible says many things. It also says ‘Fathers, do not embitter your children, or they will become discouraged.’” 43 The next morning, Kalbawi was in his usual spot inside the house. In the kitchen, her mother was making breakfast. Her anger from the night before had softened.

“I called the woman from church,” the mother said without turning around. “I told her to mind her own business.” The daughter blinked in surprise. “But,” her mother continued, turning to face her, “you will bring this boy to meet us. If you’re going to be friends with him, we need to know him. And when he comes to our home, he will see how we live, how we pray, how we honor God. He will follow our rules while he is under our roof. Understood?”

“You want to meet him?” “I want to know who has captured my daughter’s attention. Your father is right. Forbidding you will only drive you away. But,” her voice grew firm, “if this boy disrespects our faith or encourages you away from Christ, the friendship ends. Clear?” The daughter nodded, stunned. 44 Later, at the fish farm, the daughter watched Kalbawi patrol the pond’s edge. “Look at him,” the father said, appearing beside her with fresh feed. He watched Kalbawi pace, pause, and glance back at them. “Doing his job, but still keeping an eye on us.”

“Is that what I need to learn? That I can have friends outside church and still have faith?” “Maybe. Or maybe you need to learn what Kalbawi really learned: that when you wander off, when you disobey, when you fail at what you’re supposed to do, you’re not cast out. You’re corrected, yes, and guided back. But you’re still loved. Still part of the family.” That afternoon, a text came from the boy: “Movie was fun. Want to study for chemistry together tomorrow?”

The daughter looked at it for a long moment. Then she typed: “My parents want to meet you. You could come for dinner Friday if you want. Fair warning: my mom will probably interrogate you about everything, and my dad will just stare at you while our guard dog decides if you’re a threat.” 45 “Sounds terrifying. What time?” She smiled despite herself. “Seven. And it’s a Christian house. There will be prayers before dinner.” “Okay. Should I wear a cross?”

“Please don’t. Just be yourself.” “That’s what got me in trouble in the first place.” The daughter put down her phone and walked to where Kalbawi was standing guard. He looked up at her. His tail wagged slowly. And as he lifted his paw toward her, she said gently, “I’m okay. Go do your job.” And for the first time in weeks, she actually meant it. She wasn’t sure what would happen with the boy. Whether friendship would become more or naturally fade. Whether her mother would approve or her faith would waver or strengthen.

But she understood now what Paul meant. The war between what we should do and what we want to do is part of being human. It’s not the struggle that defines us; it’s how we handle it, and whether we accept grace when we fail. 46 Kalbawi barked suddenly, chasing away another heron. 47

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: Pia stood at the edge of the yard. His ears were pitched forward and his body was still. Somewhere beyond the fence line, down near the creek, the howling had started again. The daughter watched him from the porch of their small house on the reservation. She was home on break from the university. The autumn wind came down from the Eastern Sierra and carried the smell of sage and the distant sound of the pack. Pia’s whole body seemed to pull toward that sound. He lifted one front paw, then set it back down. His tail lifted slightly.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: But he stayed where he was. Her father came out with two mugs of coffee and handed one to her. He worked for Tribal Social Services during the week, but on weekends he was just her father, who made fry bread on Saturday 48 mornings and taught her the old songs alongside the hymns. “He still hears them,” she said. “Every night.” Her father sat beside her on the porch step. “Watch his ears.” Pia’s ears rotated toward the howling, then back toward the house, then toward the sound again. His tail dropped. He turned and walked back toward the porch, settled at her father’s feet, and let out a long sigh.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “What do you think he’s feeling?” she asked. “Couldn’t tell you. He’s a dog.” Her father scratched behind Pia’s ears. “But I remember when he used to run with them. That pack of rez dogs down by the creek.” She remembered too. Pia had been younger then, rangier, always disappearing at dusk and coming back at dawn smelling like mud and fish. The pack had been a motley group, mixed breeds running loose across the reservation the way rez dogs did.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “What made him stop?” 49 Her father was quiet for a moment. “The neighbor’s chickens.” She nodded slowly. She’d heard this story before, but tonight she found herself listening more carefully. She was sitting on the porch during her first visit home since the semester started. She’d been carrying questions for weeks, and she needed answers. “The pack killed three chickens before the rancher came out with his rifle,” her father said. “Shot one of them. Pia was there. He came home that night covered in blood and feathers. I don’t know if he participated or just watched. I’ll never know. But I had to make a choice.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “Keep him home.” “Keep him home.” He looked down at the dog. “He sat by this door for weeks. Every evening when the howling started, he’d scratch at the wood. Whine. Sometimes bark.” Pia’s ears perked at a distant sound, but he stayed where he was. His chin rested on his paws. “How long did it take for him to stop?” 50 “Months. I started taking him with me when I checked on the sheep.” Her father paused. “You know the neighbors we help with their flock. Pia came along on those trips. It gave him something to do. Eventually, the scratching stopped. The whining got quieter. Now he just listens.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: She thought about the Indigenous student group she’d found at the university. She thought about the way the other students had looked at her when she mentioned that her faith was important to her. Some had looked away. Others had crossed their arms. “Can I tell you something?” “Anything.” “At school, I found this group. Native students. They talk about decolonization, protecting our languages, real stuff.” She paused. “But when I told them I was Christian, everything changed. They said Christianity destroyed us. Said I was choosing the colonizer’s religion over my own people.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: Her father was quiet. Down by the creek, the howling rose and fell. 51 “So, how did you respond?” “I tried to explain. That Jesus wasn’t about what those boarding schools did. That my faith isn’t about erasing who I am.” She looked at Pia. He was still lying at their feet. “They didn’t want to hear it. One girl’s grandmother was beaten at a boarding school for speaking Paiute. How do I argue with that?” “You don’t argue with her pain. It’s real.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “So what do I do?” Her father stood and walked to the edge of the porch. The stars were coming out over the Sierras. Their ancestors had watched these same stars for thousands of years. “Remember what happened with Pia and the pack?” “You already told me that story.” “There’s more to the story.” He turned back to her. His voice was gentle. “After I kept him home, after the weeks of scratching and whining, something changed. He started working with me

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: 52 instead of against me. The sheep, the truck rides, the purpose. But here’s what I’ve been thinking about lately.” He sat back down beside her. “I don’t actually know what changed inside him. Maybe nothing. Maybe he just got used to a new routine. Maybe he forgot about the pack, or maybe he thinks about them every single night when he hears them howling. I can’t know that. He’s a dog.” Pia’s tail moved once, slowly. “What I do know,” her father continued, “is what I see. I see a dog who stays. Who works. Who doesn’t run off anymore. Even when the howling is loud. He could easily jump the fence. I see what he does. And what he doesn’t do. But I can’t know what he’s thinking. And maybe that’s all we can ever really see in each other too.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: She thought about that. “So you’re saying I should just… stay? Even when my own people don’t understand?” 53 “I’m saying you have to decide what you’re staying for. And who you’re staying with.” He paused. “Jesus said something once that always stuck with me. He said whoever loves father or mother more than Him isn’t worthy of Him. Harsh words. But honest ones.” “That’s what scares me. Choosing Jesus feels like choosing against my people.” “Does it? Or does it feel like choosing something bigger than any one group of people?” Her father looked up at the stars. “Our ancestors watched these same stars. They knew there was something greater than themselves. I think Jesus is the answer to what they were looking for.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: The next morning, she drove with her father to the neighbors’ place to check on the sheep. Pia rode in the bed of the truck. His tongue hung out and his ears flapped in the wind. When they arrived, the sheep were scattered across the pasture. Pia jumped out of the truck and ran toward the flock. His belly nearly touched the ground as he circled wide around the scattered sheep. They bunched together as he closed in. They lowered their heads and stopped shuffling.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: 54 “Look at him,” her father said. She watched Pia guide the sheep toward the water. His movements were quick and efficient, nothing like the anxious pacing he’d done at the door those first weeks. “When he ran with the pack, what did they do?” her father asked. “Knocked over trash cans. Chased cattle. Got shot at. This dog does more good here than he ever did running wild.” She watched Pia settle at the edge of the pasture. He had stopped moving and now sat watching the flock.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “I feel like I’m supposed to choose,” she said quietly. “My people or my faith. Like I can’t have both.” “Who told you that?” “Everyone. The students in the group say Christianity destroyed us. And sometimes at church, people act like our traditions are all just paganism to be left behind.” 55 Her father was quiet for a long moment. “Do you remember what Numu means?” “The people.” “The people. That’s who we are. And Jesus? He came for all people. He was born under Roman occupation. He knew what it was like to have His people oppressed.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: A hawk circled overhead. Pia had moved the sheep to the water and sat watching them. His ears were up but his body was relaxed. “So what do I do when they say I’m betraying my people by following Jesus?” “You love them anyway. You stay connected anyway. You trust that Jesus is worth following even when your own community doesn’t understand.” She went back to school. She kept attending the Indigenous student group meetings, even when the tension grew. When she mentioned praying about an issue, some of the others rolled their eyes. When she declined to participate in a ceremony

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: 56 that conflicted with her faith, the group’s president pulled her aside. “You’re confused,” he said. “Christianity is the enemy. It destroyed us.” “People destroyed us,” she said. “People who claimed Christ but didn’t follow him. The Jesus I follow was brown. He lived under Roman occupation. He stood with the oppressed.” “Pretty words. But you still choose their religion over your own culture.” That night, she sat in the library and wrestled with doubts. Was she betraying her people? Was her faith just something she’d absorbed without questioning?

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: She opened her Bible to the book of Matthew. Jesus talked about bringing not peace but a sword. He talked about the cost of following Him. The words were hard. But then she turned to John: “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.” Her phone buzzed with a video from home. Pia was in the bed of the truck beside her father. His 57 ears were up and he was watching the road ahead. The caption read: “Pia says hi. So do I.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: She smiled. She noticed when she felt most alive, most true, most connected. The answer was clear. The next group meeting focused on the boarding schools. Story after story of trauma inflicted in Christianity’s name. Her heart broke with each testimony. These were real wounds, real betrayals. When the sharing circle came to her, she stood. “My great-grandmother was in one of those schools,” she said. “They cut her hair. Beat her for speaking Paiute. Told her our ways were evil.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: Nods of solidarity around the room. “And that was evil,” she continued. “Every bit of it. But it wasn’t Jesus. It was people using His name for something He never taught. My Jesus spoke Aramaic, not English. He kept Jewish customs, not European ones. He said let the little children come to Him, not beat them into submission.” 58 She took a breath. “I follow Jesus because He transcends all cultures. He’s not a white god or a brown god. He’s the God who made everyone. And following Him has taught me that my worth isn’t in my bloodline or my grades or even my heritage. It’s in being loved by the Creator Himself.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “You’re still choosing their religion,” the president said. “I’m choosing Jesus. There’s a difference.” She gathered her things. “I love our people. I love our languages, our songs, our stories. But I love Jesus more. And that’s not betrayal. That’s freedom.” She walked out. Her hands were shaking, but her spirit was calm. Three months later, her father visited for Thanksgiving. They walked across the university campus together. Pia trotted beside them. Students stopped to admire him, but he paid them no attention. He passed other dogs near the park without reacting.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “He’s different here,” she said. “More relaxed.” 59 “New place. No pack howling at night.” They sat on a bench near the quad. Pia settled at their feet. His head was up and he watched students pass. “A few people from the group reached out to me,” she said. “Quietly. One girl’s grandmother survived a boarding school, like ours did. Despite everything, her grandmother held onto Jesus. Said the boarding school people had it all wrong. She’d been too scared to say that in meetings. But watching me speak up reminded her of her grandmother’s faith.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “So you found your people after all.” “A different group than I expected. But real.” A squirrel ran across the path. Pia’s head lifted and his body tensed for a moment. But he stayed where he was and watched it scamper up a tree. “I used to wonder what goes on in his head,” her father said. “When he hears the pack, when he sees something to chase. Does he want to go? Does he remember what it was like to run wild?”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: 60 “Do you still wonder?” “Not really. I just watch him now. A dog who stays. A dog who works. A dog who is relaxed. He eats well. He sleeps through the night.” Pia’s eyes were half-closed now. His breathing was slow. “Maybe that’s faith,” she said. “Not knowing everything, but trusting anyway. Staying even when you hear the howling.” Her father smiled. “That sounds about right.” The evening light was turning golden over the campus. Soon her father would drive back to the reservation, back to the sheep and the creek and the sound of the wild pack at dusk.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: “I’m glad you came,” she said. “Wouldn’t miss it.” He put his arm around her. “I’m proud of you, you know. Not because you agree with me about everything. But because you’re thinking for yourself. You took what we taught you and made it your own. That’s what faith is supposed to look like.” 61 Pia stood suddenly, stretching. He looked toward the parking lot, then back at her father, then toward the lot again. “He knows it’s almost time to go,” her father said. Then he caught himself. “Or maybe he just smells something. I shouldn’t pretend to know.”

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: She laughed. “You’re getting better at that.” “Your mother always said I put too many words in his mouth.” He stood and brushed off his jeans. “Ready to walk us back to the truck?” They crossed the campus together. Pia walked between them. The dog’s ears rotated at sounds and his nose worked the air. His tail swayed gently as he walked. But she knew what she was experiencing. Peace. Purpose. The strange freedom of choosing something worth more than belonging to any single group.

chunks.lesson_title: The Dog Who Left the Pack
chunks.text: The howling would start tonight back home. Pia would hear it. His ears would twitch and his body would go still. Then he would turn away and walk 62 back to the house where he was fed, where he had work to do. It looked like faithfulness. But she knew Whom she had chosen. And watching Pia climb into the truck bed, settle into his spot, and look back at her with calm eyes, she felt that was enough. More than enough.

The evening ritual was always the same at Osan Air Base. At 1700 hours, the loudspeakers crackled to life across the base. The bugle’s first notes of “Retreat” echoed off concrete buildings and chain- link fences. Everyone outdoors stopped mid-stride and turned toward the nearest flag as it descended. Inside the family’s base housing, Jax went rigid. The German Shepherd’s ears snapped forward. His breathing quickened. Every muscle in his body tensed. He paced from window to door to window again. He began to whine.

The daughter looked up from her homework at the kitchen table. “Dad, he’s doing it again.” Her twin brother paused his basketball game on TV. “Every single evening. You’d think he’d get used to it by now.” 64 “It’s okay, boy,” the daughter said softly. She reached toward Jax. But the dog’s attention stayed fixed on the door. He barked sharply, then spun in a tight circle. He’d been bred and built for military precision. He was trained to detect explosives and track threats. Now he lived in a house that smelled of teenagers’ sneakers and lavender lotion instead of gun oil and tactical gear.

The father watched from the kitchen doorway. He was still in his flight suit from another long day. He had been trying to bridge the gap between American and South Korean pilot training. “A few months since we got him, and he still does this every evening.” The mother nodded. She looked tired. She’d spent the day at the base spouse’s club, smiling and organizing events. She pretended the constant moves didn’t wear her down. “She would have known what to do,” she said. She meant Jax’s handler. They had attended her funeral several months ago.

The son emerged from his room. “Maybe we should just let him outside during retreat. Let him do his thing.” 65 “His thing used to involve live ammunition and hostile land,” the father reminded him. “I know, Dad.” The son’s voice carried an edge. “I know all about duty and rules and adapting to the mission.” The sharpness hung in the air. The son had finally made real friends at their last base before they’d been uprooted again. He’d just made the JV basketball team. He’d finally felt like somewhere might be home. Now he was the new kid again, trying to navigate Korean customs he didn’t understand and American military kids who’d formed their cliques two moves ago.

The daughter knelt beside Jax. He was pressed against the door and panting. “Hey, warrior,” she whispered. “I know. Everything’s different. Everyone expects you to just adjust, right? New place, new people, new everything. And you’re supposed to be fine because that’s what we do.” The dog’s ear twitched toward her voice, but his body remained tense against the door. “Give him space,” the mother said, though her voice lacked conviction. She knew her daughter 66 was talking to herself as much as to the dog. The daughter had inherited her mother’s gift for making everyone else comfortable while swallowing her own struggles. At school, she was already organizing study groups and joining committees. At home, the mother had heard her crying in her room at night, missing her best friend from their last base, missing the youth group at their church there, missing the life they’d built and left behind. They had done this so many times before.

The retreat ceremony ended. The base settled into evening. But Jax continued pacing. The father approached slowly, using the calm voice he’d learned from the dog behavior expert. “Jax. Heel.” The command cut through, and his military training kicked in. Although his whole body trembled, Jax obeyed. He moved to the father’s left side and sat with his shoulder aligned to the father’s knee. “Good boy,” the father said, though he knew the dog was just following orders. Jax would obey commands because that’s what he’d been trained

67 to do. Trust and belonging were different matters entirely. Later that evening, the father sat in his home office, surrounded by technical manuals. Working with the South Korean pilots was challenging. The Americans were direct, and sometimes it sounded disrespectful to the Koreans. His job was to make it work anyway. He had to adapt, bridge gaps, and be the link between two different worlds. Through the wall, he heard his son on a video call with friends from their last base. “Yeah, it’s fine. You know how it is. New base, same deal.” But the father heard the strain underneath the casual words. His son was trying so hard to embrace the military life, to make duty seem easy. The boy didn’t realize that his father’s strength came from faith, not from finding any of this easy.

The mother appeared in the doorway. “Jax won’t eat again.” Jax had lost weight since the move. The vet at the base said it was likely due to stress. “Remember what she said?” the mother asked, referring to Jax’s handler. “At that barbecue? She 68 said Jax wasn’t just a military working dog. She said he was her battle buddy, her partner. She said he knew her heart.” “And now he’s lost,” the father said. “Aren’t we all?” The mother’s voice cracked slightly. “Every couple of years we uproot everything. The kids pretend they’re fine because they see us pretending. But our daughter hasn’t even unpacked her room. She says what’s the point when we’ll just move again.”

“This is the life we chose.” “You chose. We follow.” The words came out harder than she intended. “I’m sorry. I didn’t mean…” “You did. And you’re right.” The father stood, pulled her close. “I know it’s hard.” “Do you? Because you have your mission. The pilots, the aircraft, the rules. You have purpose wherever we go. But I start over every time. New spouse’s club, new church, new everything. And I smile and organize fundraisers and pretend that being your wife is enough identity for me.”

69 Through the window, they could see Jax in the backyard. He stood at the fence line. His ears were forward and his body was tense. He watched the edges of the yard as if threats lurked in the quiet darkness. The monsoon season arrived early. The sky over Osan turned the color of old steel, and thunder rolled across the base. Jax, who’d handled real gunfire in combat overseas, fell apart at the first lightning strike. The daughter found him wedged under the coffee table. He was shaking so hard his tags rattled against each other. His eyes were wild. His pupils were dilated. When she reached for him, he snapped at the air. She pulled her hand back.

“It’s okay,” she said. She sat cross-legged on the floor. “I’m not going anywhere.” The thunder crashed again. Jax whined and pressed himself farther back. The daughter’s phone lit up with messages from friends at their old base. They were at youth group and sent pictures. “Miss you!” they wrote. “Not the same without you!” She turned the phone face 70 down. Every friendship, every space, every moment of belonging was temporary. Why invest when you’d just lose it all again?

She looked at Jax. The warrior dog was trembling under the furniture. “You know what my dad always says?” she asked him. “He has this Bible verse he quotes when things get tough. When working with the pilots isn’t going well, when the generals are breathing down his neck, when Mom cries in the bathroom. She thinks we can’t hear her.” The thunder cracked again. Jax pressed himself farther back. “’God is our refuge and strength,’” the daughter recited, “’an ever-present help in trouble.’ Psalm 46:1. He says it means God is our home. Not a place. Not a base. Not a house. God himself is where we live.”

She lay down on her stomach and made herself small. “Your person is gone. I get it. My best friend feels gone too, even though she’s just far away. But maybe home isn’t about places or even specific people. Maybe it’s about knowing you’re loved wherever you are.” 71 Slowly, carefully, she started humming a quiet hymn from church. “Be still my soul, the Lord is on thy side…” Jax’s ears turned toward her voice. “Bear patiently the cross of grief or pain…”

The dog’s breathing slowed. “Leave to thy God to order and provide…” Thunder rumbled, but it was more distant now. Jax crawled forward an inch, then two. “In every change, He faithful will remain.” Jax’s head emerged from under the table. The daughter held perfectly still as he army-crawled toward her. He rested his massive head on her outstretched arm. His body still shook, but he pressed against her. The father and mother stood frozen in the doorway and watched. The son appeared behind them. His basketball was forgotten.

“How did you do that?” the son whispered. 72 The daughter didn’t look up. She was afraid to break the moment. “I didn’t do anything. I just stayed. Sometimes that’s all anyone needs. Someone to stay when everything else keeps changing.” The father thought of his friend, Jax’s handler. He remembered how she’d talked about the dog. “He knows my heart,” she’d said. Maybe that’s what they’d all forgotten. They were always moving. They were always adapting. They needed to feel known. Moving together wasn’t enough.

Over the following weeks, the family developed new routines. Jax still tensed during retreat, but now the daughter would sit with him and hum hymns. She stayed until the ceremony ended. His handler had mentioned that Jax liked warm broth. So the mother began adding warm broth to his food. And he started eating better. The son taught him tricks in the backyard. These gave the working dog new tasks that didn’t involve detecting threats. The father began bringing Jax to the flight line occasionally. Jax wasn’t there to work. He was just there to be around the purposeful energy of aircraft and people. The South Korean pilots were fascinated by the military dog, and somehow Jax

73 became a bridge. Conversations that had been stilted became easier with the dog as a focal point. “He is warrior,” one Korean pilot said in careful English. He watched Jax execute a perfect heel. “But also he is family?” “Both,” the father replied. He watched Jax settle calmly among the pilots. The dog accepted their attention without the anxious watchfulness of those first weeks. Maybe that was what healing looked like. Jax was both warrior and family. One evening, the mother was video-calling with the spouse’s group from their last base when Jax wandered into frame. “Oh my goodness, is that her dog?” someone asked, meaning the handler.

“He’s our dog now,” the mother said. And for the first time, it felt true. The daughter had started unpacking her room, finally. “Not because I think we’re staying,” she told her mother. “But because wherever we are, we should actually live there. Not just exist waiting for the next move.” 74 The son joined the base basketball team. He hadn’t forgotten his friends from before. But he had realized that you could honor what was lost while still engaging with what was present.

The verse the father had quoted so often took on new meaning. God as refuge didn’t mean God would stop the moves, stop the changes, stop the losses. It meant He was the constant presence through them all. He was the home that traveled with them. Several months into their tour at Osan, Jax still perked up at certain sounds and scents. His ears would lift. His body would orient toward something only he detected. But he also sprawled across the daughter’s bed during homework. He brought his ball to the son for fetch. He leaned against the mother’s legs while she cooked. He walked beside the father with steady presence.

During one beautiful sunset, the family sat on their back porch. Jax lay between them. His head rested on the daughter’s lap. His body leaned against the father’s chair. His tail stretched across the son’s feet. 75 “She would be happy,” the mother said suddenly. “Seeing him like this. Knowing he’s loved.” “We should learn from him,” the daughter said. “Carrying the good parts forward. The love, the training, the purpose. But also learning new things.” The father reached for his worn Bible and flipped to Psalm 46. But instead of reading his usual verse, he continued to verse 10: “Be still, and know that I am God.”

The father thought about those words. They needed to be still. They needed to stop worrying about the next move and where they would go next. Home wasn’t a place. Home was a presence. Jax sighed deeply and relaxed. The next morning at retreat, he still stiffened at the bugle’s first notes. His training would always be part of him. But now the family helped him through it. The daughter put her hand on his head, and the father spoke to him softly. Then the son tossed him a tennis ball. And when he brought it back, the mother gave him a treat.

76 The family would move again. Orders would come, boxes would be packed, goodbyes would be said. But they’d learned that home wasn’t about places. It was about God. He was their refuge and strength. He was with them wherever they went. 77

A Saluki named Zozo lived with a family in Beirut, Lebanon. The mother walked him every evening through the narrow streets of their neighborhood. Zozo was tall and lean. He was built for speed. His ancestors had chased gazelles across the Arabian desert. Now he walked carefully between cars and around trash bins. His long stride had no room to stretch. The mother watched him pull against the leash. She knew what he was made for. He was made for open spaces, long runs, and the hunt. But they lived in a city, and open spaces were hard to find.

Tonight the mother walked slower than usual. Her mind was somewhere else. Her daughter had received two acceptance letters. One was from a university here in Beirut. The other was from a university in England. The daughter wanted to stay in Lebanon. The mother wanted her to go. 78 “You should see the world,” the mother had told her the night before. “Don’t stay here because of us.” “I’m not staying because of you,” the daughter had said. “I’m staying because I want to. Because this is home. Because you and Baba need me.”

The mother had quoted Scripture. “Honor your father and mother,” she had said. She meant it as a command to obey. She wanted her daughter to leave and take the opportunity she herself never had. But the daughter had looked at her calmly. “I am honoring you,” she said. “By choosing to stay and care for you, the way you cared for your parents.” The conversation had ended badly, and the mother had walked out of the room. She was still thinking about those words from the previous night as she walked through the streets with Zozo.

The father was home. He sat at his desk and worked on his computer. A stray bullet had hit his leg during the war in 2006, and he walked with a cane now. He spent his days writing articles about peace and hope for Lebanon. He never 79 complained about what he had lost, but the mother knew the pain he carried. They reached a small field at the edge of the neighborhood. Although apartment buildings surrounded it on all sides, it was the only open space nearby.

The mother unclipped the leash. Zozo ran. His body stretched low to the ground, and his legs moved so fast they blurred. For a few minutes, he was what God had made him to be. He was a hunter and a runner, and he was free. Then something moved in the grass, and Zozo stopped. His body changed. His muscles tensed and his head dropped low. He crept forward, one slow step at a time. The mother watched. She had seen this before. Zozo’s instinct was strong, and when he spotted prey, everything else disappeared.

He pounced. When he lifted his head, he held a small brown hedgehog in his mouth. It had curled into a tight ball. 80 “Zozo, no!” The mother started toward him. But Zozo did not bite down. He stood perfectly still, and the hedgehog stayed curled in his jaws. He could have crushed it easily. This was what he was made for: the chase, the catch, and the kill. Then he lowered his head slowly and carefully. He placed the hedgehog on the grass. The small creature stayed frozen for a moment, and then it uncurled and ran into the bushes.

Zozo watched it go. Then he turned and trotted back to the mother. She sat down on a nearby bench, and tears came to her eyes. The dog had done what she could not do. He had caught his prize and let it go. That afternoon, the mother went to the cemetery. Zozo walked beside her. She stood before her parents’ graves and placed flowers on the marble. Her parents had lived through the civil war. They had watched their friends leave the country one by one, but they had stayed. They had kept their faith and their love for Lebanon.

81 “I wanted to leave,” the mother said to the headstones. “You knew that. Every day after university, I talked about Paris. London. New York. You listened. You nodded. And then you would say, ‘But who will care for Lebanon if all the educated people leave? Who will preserve our faith and our community?’” She had stayed. At first, she stayed out of duty and guilt. Her father’s heart was weak, and her mother had diabetes. She believed that good daughters do not abandon their parents. She met her future husband at church, married him, and raised the daughter here. She had built a life she did not choose.

But she had grown to love it. “I resented you,” she whispered. “Even after you died, I resented you for keeping me here.” She paused. “And now my daughter is doing the same thing. But she is not staying from duty. She is staying from love. She sees her father’s pain. She knows I cannot manage alone. And she is choosing us. Choosing Lebanon.” Zozo pressed his nose against her hand. She scratched behind his ears. 82 “You would have loved her choice,” she told her parents. “You always quoted Psalm 127. ‘Children are a heritage from the Lord. A reward from Him.’ I thought that meant children should obey. But maybe it means children are God’s gift to us. Not our possessions to control.”

That evening, the mother found the daughter on the balcony. Books about peace and conflict were spread across the small table. “I went to see your grandparents today,” the mother said. The daughter looked up, surprised. “I wanted to apologize to them. For my resentment. For blaming them all these years for making me stay.” She took a breath. “And I realized I was doing the same thing to you. They tried to make me stay. I have been trying to make you leave. But both of us were wrong. The choice should be yours.”

“Mama?” “Zozo caught a hedgehog today. He held it in his mouth. Everything in him said to bite down. But 83 he let it go. He placed it gently on the ground and watched it leave.” The mother reached for her daughter’s hand. “You are not a hedgehog, and I am not a hunter. But I have been holding you in my jaws. Trying to force you to do what I wanted to do. Run away. Because I could not.”

“I don’t want to run, Mama.” “I know. And that is the miracle. I stayed because I felt I had to. You’re staying because you want to.” The daughter’s eyes filled with tears. “I might still leave someday. For graduate school. Or work.” “Or you might not. Either way, it will be your choice. Not my dream forced onto you.” They sat together and watched the sun set over the city. Beirut spread below them. The city was beautiful, ancient, and modern.

The father joined them, moving slowly with his cane. “What are you two talking about?” “Peace,” the daughter said, and smiled at her mother. 84 The father smiled too and eased into a chair. That Sunday at church, the family stood together during the service. The priest read from Psalm 127. “Children are a heritage from the Lord. A reward from Him. Like arrows in the hands of a warrior are children born in one’s youth.” The mother thought about arrows. Arrows are meant to be aimed and released. They are not meant to be kept in the quiver forever.

After church, they took Zozo to the field again. This time the daughter came too. They watched him run. “He is beautiful,” the daughter said. “He was made for the wide open desert,” the mother said. “But he has found joy even here.” “Like you, Mama. You found joy.” The mother pulled her daughter close. “Your grandparents would be proud of you. Not because you are staying. But because you are choosing to follow your own path with God.” 85

Zozo ran until he was tired, and then he trotted back to them. He lay down at their feet. His tongue hung out and his sides moved with heavy breaths. He had run for the joy of running. The mother looked at him. He had been made for the desert, for the hunt, and for wide open spaces. But he had found his place here, in a small field in Beirut, with this family. And so had she. 86 Final Thoughts

If you have made it through all twelve lessons, thank you for staying with us. Thank you for walking through these stories of dogs and families trying to figure out what really matters. We did not write this book because we have answers. We wrote it because we have walked similar paths ourselves. We know what it feels like to carry expectations that seem too heavy. We know the exhaustion that comes from always being “on.” And we know the relief that comes when someone finally sees us for who we are instead of what we represent.

Through these twelve lessons, we met families from different backgrounds and cultures. We watched a Shiba Inu named Kiko become tense and withdrawn because he could not stop performing for guests. We saw a German Shepherd whose rescue dog temperament did not match his expected lineage. We followed Naia, a chocolate Lab whose family’s reputation made real friendships almost impossible for her young owner to find. 87 We met a Basset Hound whose family had learned to hide their struggles behind a perfect image. We watched Bori, an Australian Shepherd, pace restlessly as her missionary family tried to adjust to life in Thailand. We sat beside Scout, a Border Collie, as his family learned that their son was more than his baseball statistics.

We observed Ghost, a Great Pyrenees, sitting beside a girl who felt invisible in the shadow of her parents’ achievements. We watched Atlas, a Bernese Mountain Dog, position himself beside whichever family member was hurting most. We followed Kalbawi, an Armant sheepdog in Egypt, as his family learned that guilt was not the same as faithfulness. We walked with a dog who had to leave his pack to find his own calling. We welcomed Jax, a military working dog, as a family learned from him that home was not about geography. And we sat on a rooftop in Beirut with Zozo, a Saluki, as his owner learned to release what she could not control.

We met twelve dogs and twelve families, each facing a different version of the same struggle. 88 We saw the pressure to perform. We saw the expectation to follow the family path. We saw the loneliness of friendships that feel conditional and the exhaustion of hiding our struggles to protect the family name. We saw the anxiety that comes from moving again and again and the burden of being measured and evaluated. We saw the pain of feeling invisible and the responsibility of being the “good” one. We saw the guilt of being imperfect, the conflict of wanting something different, the challenge of finding rest, and the difficulty of letting go.

These struggles are real. They are not small. And they deserve to be named out loud. If you are living under the pressure of your family’s expectations right now, whether those expectations come from cultural heritage, religious tradition, professional reputation, or dreams your parents have carried for you since before you were born, know this: the people who love you, even the ones putting pressure on you, usually do so because they want good things for you. They may not realize what that pressure is costing. They may not see the anxiety or the isolation or the exhaustion. They may not understand that the

89 path they have imagined for you might not be the path God has prepared. From watching dogs, we have learned that they do not care about pedigree. A purebred and a shelter mutt will play together without checking credentials. A show dog will be happiest rolling in mud. A working dog bred for one purpose may find joy in something completely different. God works the same way, only deeper and more completely. The Bible tells us that God does not look at outward appearances. He looks at the heart. He does not check your family tree before offering His love. He does not measure your worth by how well you fulfill cultural expectations or preserve family traditions.

In the story of the prodigal son, Jesus described a father who ran to embrace his returning child before that child had accomplished anything, proven anything, or earned anything. The father’s love was not a reward for success. It was simply there, waiting, unconditional. 90 And in the same story, Jesus described an older brother who had done everything right. He had followed every rule, met every expectation, and never strayed from the family path. But he was so focused on performance that he missed the celebration happening right in front of him.

Many of us are like that older brother. We work so hard to earn love that we forget it was never something we had to earn in the first place. Heritage is a gift. Family traditions can be beautiful. Cultural values connect us to something larger than ourselves. But when these things become the only measure of our worth, when they leave no room for our individual calling, when they demand perfection rather than growth, they can crush the very people they were meant to bless.

So whether you are the child of immigrants carrying the weight of their sacrifice, the heir to generations of family business, the one expected to follow religious footsteps, or simply someone who dreams differently than your family dreamed for you, we hope these stories have helped you breathe a little easier. 91 Your story can honor your family’s story without repeating it. Your path can respect your heritage while also expanding it. Your choices can show gratitude for where you came from while also embracing where you are going.

The families we wrote about did not find easy answers. Real family dynamics are complicated. Cultural expectations do not disappear overnight. Generational patterns take time to change. But in each story, we saw families begin to change. We saw honest conversations begin. We saw new understanding develop. We saw grace extended and received. That is what we pray for you. We do not pray that all pressure will disappear or that your family will suddenly understand everything. We pray for small steps and moments of honesty. We pray for the courage to name what you are feeling. We pray for people who see you as you really are. And above all, we pray for the deep assurance that your worth was never determined by your pedigree.

It never was. God sees you. He knows you. He loves you. He loves you in your questions and in your certainty. 92 He loves you whether you follow the path your family has laid out for you or forge your own. He loves you when you do what your family hoped you would do and when you fall short. His love does not depend on what you preserve or what you become. You are already enough. You always have been.

So take a breath. Let your shoulders drop. You do not have to perform today. You do not have to represent anyone but yourself. You are not a brand to be managed or a legacy to be protected. You are a person, a child of God, deeply loved. And that love is available to you right now, exactly as you are. 93

We are just a regular person and a local church deacon. The only thing truly worth mentioning is this: we’ve been forgiven. God has given us His Spirit to walk through this life and to slowly, steadily become more like Jesus. He gave His life for us on the cross, and in doing so, gave us life, now and forever. Jesus is our Savior, our Lord, and the reason we get out of bed every morning. 94
